papers.paper: AlexNet — "ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks" (Krizhevsky, Sutskever & Hinton, 2012)
papers.keyIdea: A generic deep neural network trained end-to-end on GPUs crushed a decade of hand-engineered computer vision pipelines. The durable lesson is not the architecture (CNNs were 20 years old) but the proof that general learning methods + more compute + more data beat domain-expert feature engineering — the 'bitter lesson' demonstrated as a single benchmark result, and the starting gun for everything else on this timeline.
papers.keyArtifact: The ILSVRC-2012 leaderboard: 15.3% top-5 error vs 26.2% for the second-best entry — a 10.9-point gap in a competition where annual progress had been 1–2 points. Comprehensible because it is literally two percentages on the same test: no math required, the gap IS the argument. Pair it with the hardware footnote that lands with engineers: trained in ~a week on two consumer GTX 580 gaming GPUs (3GB each).
papers.builderTakeaway: Bet on general methods that scale with compute/data over clever hand-tuned pipelines — every subsequent paper on this site is that bet repeated. Practically: paradigm shifts announce themselves as discontinuities on public benchmarks, not increments; when you see a 10-point jump instead of a 1-point jump, reprioritize.
papers.commonMisconception: "AlexNet invented neural networks / deep learning." CNNs existed since the 1980s (LeCun's LeNet read ZIP codes in 1989). What changed in 2012 was that ImageNet's 1.2M labeled images, GPU compute, and a few training tricks (ReLU, dropout) finally gave the old idea enough fuel — the invention was scale, not the network.
papers.honestAnalogy: Like fracking: the oil (neural nets) and where it sat were known for decades; a new extraction technology (GPU training at scale) suddenly made it economical, and the entire industry reorganized around it within two years.

papers.paper: ResNet — "Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition" (He et al., 2015)
papers.keyIdea: Very deep networks failed to train not from overfitting but from optimization failure — each layer had to re-derive everything the previous layers knew. ResNet reframes every layer as learning only the change (residual) to apply to its input via an identity skip connection: output = F(x) + x. Depth becomes nearly free, and that skip connection now sits inside every Transformer block powering the models this audience uses daily.
papers.keyArtifact: The one-line residual equation y = F(x) + x, motivated by the paradox in the paper's Figure 1: a plain 56-layer network has HIGHER training error than a 20-layer one — so it isn't overfitting, the optimizer literally can't find the deeper solution. Comprehensible because '+ x' is the entire trick: just add the input back; any engineer instantly recognizes a pass-through default. Supporting numbers: 152 layers, 3.57% top-5 error, swept 1st place at ILSVRC 2015.
papers.builderTakeaway: The 'residual stream' you hear about in interpretability work is literally this +x path. Design lesson that generalizes to pipelines and agent chains: when quality degrades as you add stages, make pass-through the default and have each stage contribute only a delta, instead of forcing every stage to reconstruct the whole state.
papers.commonMisconception: That deep nets failed because of overfitting (too many parameters) or purely vanishing gradients. The paper's own evidence: training error itself got worse with depth — an optimization problem. Skip connections fix it by making 'do nothing' trivially learnable, so extra depth can only help.
papers.honestAnalogy: A document passing through 50 editors where each editor receives the current draft and only adds tracked changes — versus each editor retyping the whole document from memory. With tracked changes, nothing is lost by default and each stage only has to add value.

papers.paper: Transformer — "Attention Is All You Need" (Vaswani et al., 2017)
papers.keyIdea: Delete recurrence entirely; let every token compute a weighted lookup over all other tokens, all in parallel. Parallel training over the whole sequence is what made scaling to today's LLMs physically and economically possible. A 'context window' is literally the set of tokens participating in this lookup.
papers.keyArtifact: Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T/√d_k)·V — the single equation executing billions of times per second in every model the reader uses. Comprehensible because it reads as a fuzzy database query: Q = what this token is looking for, K = what each token advertises, softmax turns match scores into weights, and the output is a weighted sum of V = what each token carries. Supporting numbers from the abstract: 28.4 BLEU on WMT-14 En-De (beating ensembles) after only 3.5 days on eight GPUs — better AND cheaper is why everyone switched.
papers.builderTakeaway: This equation is your API bill: QK^T compares every token against every other, so attention cost grows roughly quadratically with context — which is why long prompts are expensive, why KV/prompt caching exists, and why retrieval usually beats context-stuffing. Understanding 'each new token attends over all previous ones' demystifies mid-context forgetting and latency behavior.
papers.commonMisconception: "This paper invented attention." Attention existed since 2014 (Bahdanau), bolted onto RNNs. The radical move was removing the recurrence and keeping ONLY attention, which unlocked parallelism. Also: it's a machine-translation paper — nobody in it predicted chatbots; the generality was discovered later.
papers.honestAnalogy: Writing the next word of a summary while being able to glance back at every word of the source, with a highlighter automatically weighting how relevant each source word is to the word you're writing right now. The RNN it replaced had to read the source once and write from a fixed-size memory.

papers.paper: GPT-2 — "Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners" (Radford et al., 2019)
papers.keyIdea: One dumb objective — predict the next token on 40GB of web text — yields a model that does translation, summarization, and QA with zero task-specific training. The bet that defined the next five years: tasks are latent in the distribution of human text, so modeling text well means implicitly learning the tasks embedded in it.
papers.keyArtifact: The paper's Figure 1: four panels (reading comprehension, translation, summarization, QA) where zero-shot performance climbs steadily with model size from 117M → 1542M parameters, with dashed reference lines showing where dedicated supervised systems sit. Comprehensible because each panel is just 'line goes up as the model gets bigger, with no task training at all' — the visual thesis GPT-3 later proved at scale. Supporting claim from the abstract: state of the art on 7 of 8 language-modeling datasets, zero-shot, while still underfitting WebText.
papers.builderTakeaway: This is the origin of the modern builder's default question: not 'how do I train a model for task X' but 'how do I ELICIT task X from a general model.' Capabilities you didn't build are already in the model. It's also the origin of release-norms debates — the staged release and 'too dangerous to release' episode started the playbook every lab now follows.
papers.commonMisconception: "GPT-2 was trained to chat or follow instructions." It was trained only to continue text. It performed tasks because demonstrations of tasks occur naturally in web text (e.g. 'translated into French: ...'). Instruction-following didn't exist until RLHF, three years later.
papers.honestAnalogy: Discovering that someone hired only to finish other people's sentences had — in order to get genuinely good at that on the whole internet — quietly learned to translate, summarize, and answer trivia, because you cannot reliably finish humanity's sentences without those skills.

papers.paper: Scaling Laws — "Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models" (Kaplan et al., 2020)
papers.keyIdea: LLM loss falls as a smooth, predictable power law in model size, dataset size, and compute — holding over up to eight orders of magnitude, with architectural details mattering far less than scale. Capability became a purchasable, forecastable commodity, which is why billion-dollar training runs are rational capital allocation rather than gambles.
papers.keyArtifact: L(N) = (Nc/N)^0.076 with Nc ≈ 8.8×10^13 (siblings: α_D ≈ 0.095 for data, α_C ≈ 0.050 for compute) — rendered as the paper's Figure 1: three dead-straight lines on log-log axes spanning eight orders of magnitude of compute. Comprehensible because nobody needs to parse the exponent — the straight lines ARE the message: run small cheap experiments, put a ruler through the points, and read off what a 1000x bigger run will deliver before writing the check. This is the artifact the interactive slider widget animates.
papers.builderTakeaway: 'Wait for the next model' is a real strategy with a rough price and date attached — don't build a company whose entire value is compensating for a weakness one more order of magnitude erases. Know the epilogue for credibility: Chinchilla (2022) corrected the coefficients (Kaplan-era models were data-starved; you should scale data with params), but the power-law framework itself survived and still schedules the industry.
papers.commonMisconception: Either "scaling has hit a wall" or "scaling laws guarantee AGI." The laws predict LOSS on next-token prediction, not specific abilities; the mapping from smooth loss curves to capabilities is not in the equation — individual capabilities can jump discontinuously while loss falls smoothly, in both exciting and unnerving ways.
papers.honestAnalogy: Moore's Law for capability: an empirical regularity, not a law of physics — yet reliable enough that an entire industry has scheduled products, GPU purchases, and fundraising rounds around it for years.

papers.paper: GPT-3 — "Language Models are Few-Shot Learners" (Brown et al., 2020)
papers.keyIdea: At 175B parameters, a pure next-token predictor can learn a new task from examples placed in the prompt — zero gradient updates. 'In-context learning' turned the prompt into a programming interface; it is the direct ancestor of prompt engineering, few-shot prompting, and ultimately of treating English as a runtime.
papers.keyArtifact: Figure 1.2: task accuracy vs number of in-context examples, one curve per model size — small models stay flat, the 175B curve climbs steeply as examples are added. Comprehensible because every reader has personally lived this ('adding examples to my prompt made it better'); the figure reveals that experience is an emergent property of scale that nobody programmed. Supporting number: 175B parameters, ~10x anything before it.
papers.builderTakeaway: Few-shot examples remain the cheapest quality lever you have — try k good examples in the prompt before reaching for fine-tuning. In-context learning is also the mechanism that makes RAG work at all: retrieved text changes model behavior at inference time with no training. Budget note: those examples are paid tokens on every call — exactly what prompt caching amortizes.
papers.commonMisconception: "The model learns from my prompts / remembers what I taught it." In-context learning updates zero weights; nothing persists after the request. It is conditioning, not training — every call starts from the same frozen model, which is also why your examples must be re-sent (or cached) each time.
papers.honestAnalogy: A vastly experienced contractor who has already seen every kind of job: show two or three examples of the format you want and they instantly produce a fourth in the same style — but they retain nothing when they leave, and next visit you must show the examples again.

papers.keyIdea: Decouple parameter count from compute per token: replace each dense feed-forward layer with many parallel 'expert' FFNs plus a small learned router that sends each token to exactly ONE expert. Total parameters can scale to the trillions while FLOPs per token stay constant — the economic trick behind most frontier models (Mixtral, DeepSeek-V3, and reportedly GPT-4).
papers.keyArtifact: The abstract's headline — up to 7x pre-training speedup over T5-Base at the SAME computational budget — paired with the routing diagram (paper Figure 2): a token arrives, the router picks 1 of N expert blocks, the rest sit idle. Comprehensible because the diagram is a load balancer: every engineer has drawn this exact box-and-arrows picture for microservices, and the 7x states the payoff in a single figure of merit.
papers.builderTakeaway: 'Parameters' and 'inference cost' are no longer the same axis. An MoE model with hundreds of billions of total parameters can cost like a small dense model per token — so when comparing models on price/speed/capability, ask about ACTIVE parameters, not headline size. This decoupling is a large part of why API prices keep falling while capability rises, and why open MoE releases undercut closed dense pricing.
papers.commonMisconception: "Experts are human-legible domain specialists — one does code, one does law." Routing is per-token and learned under load-balancing pressure; observed specialization is mostly token-level patterns (punctuation, syntax, language), not subjects. And MoE is not an ensemble of independent models — experts are interleaved inside every layer of one network.
papers.honestAnalogy: A hospital triage desk: each patient (token) is routed to one appropriate specialist instead of being examined by every doctor on staff. You can grow the staff — the institution's total knowledge — 100x without any individual visit taking longer. The real cost is the new operations problem: keeping load balanced so one doctor isn't slammed while others idle.

papers.paper: InstructGPT / RLHF — "Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback" (Ouyang et al., 2022)
papers.keyIdea: A raw language model predicts text; it doesn't try to help you. RLHF adds the product layer in three steps: (1) supervised fine-tuning on human demonstrations, (2) train a reward model from human rankings of model outputs, (3) optimize the LM against that reward model with RL (PPO). This is the step that turned the GPT-3 curiosity into the assistant products everyone now uses — alignment as a product layer, not an afterthought.
papers.keyArtifact: Two-part artifact, both simple: the paper's 3-step pipeline diagram (SFT → reward model → PPO — the most-redrawn diagram in the industry, the site should show it faithfully), and the abstract's headline result: human labelers preferred outputs from the 1.3B-parameter InstructGPT over the 175B GPT-3 — 100x fewer parameters. Comprehensible because the diagram is three boxes and the number kills the 'bigger is always better' intuition in one comparison.
papers.builderTakeaway: Model quality as users experience it is substantially post-training, not scale — which is why preference-tuning on YOUR task can beat upgrading to a bigger model, and why models from different vendors 'feel' different despite similar pretraining: you are feeling the reward model. Sycophancy, over-hedging, refusal style are RLHF artifacts — build evals for them explicitly.
papers.commonMisconception: "RLHF makes models smarter / adds knowledge." It adds essentially no knowledge; it reshapes behavior — instruction-following, format, tone, refusals. The paper even measures an 'alignment tax' (regressions on some benchmarks, which they mitigate). RLHF is steering, not capability.
papers.honestAnalogy: Media training for a brilliant but rambling expert: they learn no new facts, but they learn to answer the question actually asked, in the expected format, and to decline appropriately instead of freewheeling. And sometimes the training makes them measurably blander — that trade-off is real, named, and measured in the paper.

papers.paper: Constitutional AI — "Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback" (Bai et al., Anthropic, 2022)
papers.keyIdea: Replace tens of thousands of human harmlessness labels with a short written list of principles — a constitution — that the model applies to itself. Phase 1: the model critiques and revises its own harmful outputs, and is fine-tuned on the revisions. Phase 2 (RLAIF): an AI compares response pairs against the principles, a preference model is trained on those AI judgments, and RL runs against it. Humans still label helpfulness only. Model values become explicit, inspectable text — this is why Claude behaves and refuses the way it does.
papers.keyArtifact: The paper's Figure 1 two-phase pipeline diagram (critique → revision → SFT, then AI comparisons → preference model → RL), anchored by one number: the constitution in the paper is just 16 plain-English principles. Comprehensible because the principles are literally readable sentences ('choose the response that is less harmful...') — governance as a config file, with no math anywhere in the loop. Show 2–3 real principles verbatim.
papers.builderTakeaway: Supervising a model with another model plus explicit written rules is now the standard pattern — LLM-as-judge, self-critique loops, policy-in-the-prompt all descend from this. If you're building agent guardrails, write your constitution as testable natural-language principles rather than accumulating one-off human labels: it scales, it's auditable, and you can diff it. Also decodes vendor differences: refusal style is a policy-document choice, not an inevitability of the tech.
papers.commonMisconception: "Constitutional AI means no humans / the AI chooses its own values." Humans wrote the constitution and still provide all helpfulness labels; what got automated is the application of the rules at scale, not the choice of them. Second correction: per the paper's Elo results (Figure 2), RL-CAI models were less harmful at a given level of helpfulness — safety here was not purchased with more refusals.
papers.honestAnalogy: Replacing case-by-case moderation calls with a written editorial policy that a junior editor (the model itself) applies to every draft. Humans still author the policy and remain accountable for it; what scales is enforcement — and unlike 50,000 opaque thumbs-up/down labels, you can read the policy and argue with a specific clause.

papers.paper: Chain-of-Thought — "Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models" (Wei et al., 2022)
papers.keyIdea: Adding worked step-by-step examples to the prompt makes large models dramatically better at multi-step problems — and the ability is emergent, only appearing around ~100B parameters (below that, CoT can hurt). The deeper principle it launched: answer quality can be bought with inference-time tokens — the seed of o1, R1, and every 'extended thinking' toggle the reader uses today.
papers.keyArtifact: One before/after bar: PaLM 540B on GSM8K math word problems goes 17.9% → 56.9% solve rate — same model, same weights, zero training; only the prompt changed. Comprehensible because it's a 3x improvement from a change the reader could type themselves. Show alongside it the paper's actual exemplar verbatim ('Roger has 5 tennis balls. He buys 2 more cans... Roger started with 5 balls. 2 cans of 3 tennis balls each is 6 tennis balls. 5 + 6 = 11.') — this is the widget's side-by-side comparison content.
papers.builderTakeaway: Quality-vs-cost is now a runtime dial: more reasoning tokens = better answers = higher latency and bill. That's literally the dial you set with reasoning-effort/extended-thinking settings. Product design follows: route easy queries to fast cheap paths, spend tokens only on hard ones, and meter it. Caveat that saves money: below a capability threshold (small/cheap models), CoT-style prompting can make results worse, not better.
papers.commonMisconception: "The printed reasoning is a faithful transcript of how the model computed its answer." CoT correlates with accuracy but can be post-hoc rationalization — treat it as useful scaffolding and a debugging signal, not ground-truth introspection. Also: CoT was a discovered PROMPTING behavior; models weren't trained to reason step-by-step until years later (R1/o1).
papers.honestAnalogy: The difference between demanding an instant answer to a multi-digit multiplication and handing someone scratch paper. The scratch paper doesn't make anyone smarter — it externalizes intermediate state so errors stop compounding. And scratch paper only helps someone who already knows how to multiply: small models just scribble fluent nonsense.

papers.paper: DeepSeek-R1 — "DeepSeek-R1: Incentivizing Reasoning Capability in LLMs via Reinforcement Learning" (DeepSeek-AI, 2025)
papers.keyIdea: Reasoning can be trained with pure reinforcement learning against automatically checkable rewards — no human-written reasoning traces. R1-Zero: take a base model, reward only 'final answer is correct' (plus a format reward for <think></think> tags), and long chains of thought, self-verification, and 'aha-moment' backtracking EMERGE rather than being taught. Released with open weights matching OpenAI's o1, it proved frontier reasoning is reproducible — capability moats are shallower than they look.
papers.keyArtifact: The AIME 2024 (competition math) trajectory: R1-Zero's pass@1 climbs from 15.6% → 71.0% over RL training with zero supervised reasoning data; the polished R1 reaches 79.8%, edging OpenAI o1-1217's 79.2%. Comprehensible because it's a single score on a single benchmark telling a complete story — nothing-up-my-sleeve RL takes a base model from 'fails' to 'matches the best closed model,' and the paper's training curve lets the reader literally watch the line go up. The RL loop to diagram: generate answer → rule-based check (correct? formatted?) → reward → update.
papers.builderTakeaway: Post-training is now where differentiation happens, and its key input is a VERIFIABLE reward. If your domain has an automatic success check — tests pass, answer matches, constraint satisfied — it is a candidate for RL-style improvement, and models will keep getting sharply better at it; if quality is a matter of taste, expect slower gains. Also: R1's distilled small models put strong reasoning at commodity prices, and the open release repriced the entire market in a week — assume any closed capability gets replicated openly within months.
papers.commonMisconception: "R1 proves you only need RL, no human data." R1-Zero alone had poor readability and language-mixing; the shipped R1 adds cold-start SFT data and multi-stage training. Second correction: the reward is not a learned reward model scoring vibes — it's rule-based checking of final answers, which is precisely why it works (a rule checker is much harder to reward-hack).
papers.honestAnalogy: Learning chess from nothing but win/loss feedback instead of studying annotated grandmaster games: given enough games, strategy and self-correction emerge because they win, not because anyone demonstrated them. The catch — and the limit of the method — is that chess has an automatic referee; this only works in domains that do.

papers.paper: CLIP — "Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision" (Radford et al., 2021)
papers.keyIdea: Train an image encoder and a text encoder jointly on 400 million web (image, caption) pairs so matching pairs land close together in one shared embedding space. Classification becomes retrieval ('which caption best fits this image?'), enabling zero-shot recognition of concepts never explicitly labeled — and establishing the shared-embedding-space pattern beneath vector search, semantic RAG retrieval, and text-to-image steering.
papers.keyArtifact: Figure 1's contrastive-training diagram: a batch of N images and N captions, an N×N similarity grid, training pushes the diagonal (true pairs) up and every off-diagonal cell down. Comprehensible because it's a matching game graspable in one glance — 'make the right caption the nearest neighbor of its image' — and the grid makes 'embedding space' concrete without any equations. Supporting number from the abstract: zero-shot CLIP matches the original supervised ResNet-50 on ImageNet using none of its 1.28M labeled training examples.
papers.builderTakeaway: 'Embed both sides into one space, then nearest-neighbor' is the workhorse pattern you already ship: semantic search, RAG retrieval, recommendations, dedup, image search. Every time you reach for a vector DB you are living downstream of CLIP's recipe. Strategic corollary: rigid label taxonomies are becoming optional — natural language is the label space, so 'add a new category' is a string, not a retraining project.
papers.commonMisconception: "CLIP generates images." It only embeds and scores. DALL-E and Stable Diffusion use CLIP(-style) text encoders to STEER generation; CLIP itself can only tell you how well an image and a text match. Related confusion worth correcting: CLIP's zero-shot 'classification' is just retrieval against candidate captions like 'a photo of a dog.'
papers.honestAnalogy: One universal card catalog where photos and sentences are filed by meaning rather than by format — a beach photo and the phrase 'sunny beach' end up on the same shelf. Once everything is shelved by meaning, search is just walking to the right shelf, and nobody had to pre-tag anything.

papers.paper: Stable Diffusion — "High-Resolution Image Synthesis with Latent Diffusion Models" (Rombach et al., 2022)
papers.keyIdea: Pixel-space diffusion is brutally expensive because most pixels are perceptual detail, not meaning. Latent diffusion first compresses the image with an autoencoder, runs the entire iterative denoising loop in that small latent space with the text prompt steering each step via cross-attention, and decodes to pixels once at the end. That efficiency collapse put generative imagery on consumer GPUs — and the open-weights release built the whole ecosystem (fine-tunes, LoRAs, ControlNet) in months.
papers.keyArtifact: The paper's Figure 3 architecture diagram, with its three visually distinct zones: pixel space (encoder/decoder) | latent space (the U-Net denoising loop) | conditioning (text → cross-attention arrows injected into every denoising step). Comprehensible because it directly answers the reader's real question — 'how does my prompt become an image?' — and the cross-attention arrows reuse the exact Attention(Q,K,V) formula from the Transformer page, rewarding readers of the earlier card. Anchor it with checkable arithmetic: a 512×512×3 image is 786,432 numbers; the 64×64×4 latent is 16,384 — ~48x less work on every one of the ~50 denoising steps.
papers.builderTakeaway: Two lessons. Technical: the biggest cost wins come from changing the SPACE you compute in, not micro-optimizing the loop — work on compressed/embedded representations (same meta-lesson as CLIP). Strategic: SD shipped open weights against a closed, better-funded DALL-E 2 and won the ecosystem within months — open weights convert your users into your R&D department. That playbook (rerun by Llama and R1) is a live option in every AI product strategy discussion the reader will sit in.
papers.commonMisconception: "Diffusion models collage pieces of training images." Generation is iterative denoising from pure random noise, steered by text — there is no lookup or pasting (memorization of near-duplicates exists as an edge case, but it is not the mechanism). Also: "Stable Diffusion invented diffusion" — DDPM (2020) invented the method; this paper made it affordable; the open release made it a movement.
papers.honestAnalogy: A sculptor working on a maquette (scale model) instead of the full marble block: every iteration of shaping happens where changes are cheap, and only the finished form is scaled up. The autoencoder is the trusted enlargement process guaranteeing the maquette expands faithfully to full size.

papers.paper: LoRA — "LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models" (Hu et al., 2021)
papers.keyIdea: The weight changes that fine-tuning makes have low intrinsic rank — so don't update the giant matrix W at all. Freeze it and learn the update as a product of two skinny matrices: W' = W + BA, with rank r as small as 1–8. Fine-tuning quality at ~1/10,000 of the trainable parameters, with the entire customization living in a small swappable file that merges into W at inference (zero added latency).
papers.keyArtifact: The decomposition h = Wx + BAx, drawn as the paper's Figure 1: the big frozen W beside a skinny trainable bypass A (r×k) and B (d×r) — plus the abstract's numbers: 10,000x fewer trainable parameters and 3x less GPU memory on GPT-3 175B, shrinking a per-task checkpoint from ~350GB to ~35MB. Comprehensible because the whole idea is matrix-shape arithmetic a reader can verify on a napkin: a full d×k update costs d·k numbers, but a rank-r version costs d·r + r·k — for d=k=4096 and r=8 that's ~0.4% of the parameters. Middle-school area math, no calculus.
papers.builderTakeaway: LoRA is the reason fine-tuning is a product feature instead of a capital expense — any API fine-tune or 'train on your data' feature you use is almost certainly LoRA-family (or quantized QLoRA). Architecture unlock worth designing around: one frozen base model + N per-customer 35MB adapters hot-swapped per request = multi-tenant customization without N model copies. Rule of thumb for choosing tools: LoRA for style/format/task behavior, RAG for knowledge.
papers.commonMisconception: "Fine-tuning (LoRA) teaches the model new knowledge." LoRA's capacity is tiny by construction — it excels at style, tone, format, and task behavior; for injecting fresh or changing facts, retrieval is usually the right tool. Also 'cheap' does not mean 'worse': the paper reports on-par or better quality than full fine-tuning on RoBERTa, DeBERTa, GPT-2, and GPT-3 across the tasks studied.
papers.honestAnalogy: Instead of re-saving a 350GB spreadsheet to share your edits, you ship a compact formula file — 'adjust these patterns by this much.' Anyone holding the original spreadsheet plus your 35MB delta reproduces your customized version exactly, and can swap between many people's deltas instantly.
